metadata.summary: Choose and provision a vector DB: Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Qdrant, Milvus, pgvector; HNSW vs IVF vs Flat; setup code; VectorStoreBase abstraction. Metric and index type are irreversible.

content.title: Vector Database Setup
content.summary: Decision methodology for choosing and provisioning a vector database at the start of a RAG project: comparison of Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Qdrant, Milvus, and pgvector; HNSW vs IVF vs Flat index selection; setup code for each provider; and a provider-agnostic VectorStoreBase abstraction. Distance metric and index type must be decided before the first upsert because both are irreversible without recreating the collection.

why.p: A wrong backend choice (Chroma for 10M vectors, wrong distance metric, no payload indexing) surfaces only at scale and requires a full re-index to fix. Selecting the right combination of hosting model, index type, and metadata strategy up-front avoids multi-day migrations. The VectorStoreBase abstraction keeps application code decoupled from the backend so future migrations require only swapping the implementation.

list.item: Starting a new RAG pipeline that needs a vector store
Migrating from one vector DB to another (e.g., Chroma → Qdrant for production)
Building multi-tenant search requiring data isolation by namespace/collection
Embedding datasets with >10K vectors where in-memory numpy arrays are no longer viable
Recommendation or duplicate-detection systems needing fast ANN at scale
Fewer than 5K vectors with no strict latency SLA — numpy cosine search is sufficient, zero infra
Relational data with complex JOINs — pgvector's SQL expressiveness matters more than ANN speed
Text search where full BM25/TF-IDF ranking is primary — use Elasticsearch or Typesense instead
Throwaway experiments with no persistence — in-memory Chroma is simpler
Pinecone: managed SaaS, serverless, auto-scale, pay-per-use — best for teams wanting zero infra ops
Qdrant: open-source, self-hosted Docker/K8s, best performance + filtering + quantization at scale
Chroma: open-source, in-process SQLite, zero infra — best for local dev and eval harnesses
Weaviate: open-source/managed, schema-first, GraphQL API, built-in vectorizer integrations
Milvus: open-source, K8s-native, enterprise scale (billions of vectors)
pgvector: PostgreSQL extension — best when the application already uses Postgres and vector count is moderate

rule.statement: Use HNSW for real-time search requiring <10ms latency and datasets up to tens of millions of vectors.
rule.rationale: HNSW achieves 95-99% recall with the lowest query latency of any ANN index. Memory overhead is ~40-50 bytes per vector per HNSW link.

rule.statement: Use IVF for memory-constrained environments where query latency of 10-50ms is acceptable.
rule.rationale: IVF divides the space into Voronoi cells; query probes only a subset of cells, trading some recall for lower memory usage than HNSW.

rule.statement: Use Flat (exact brute-force) only for datasets with fewer than 10K vectors.
rule.rationale: Flat search is O(n); at 10K vectors it remains under 10ms, but at 1M vectors it becomes prohibitively slow.

rule.statement: Choose the distance metric (cosine, L2, dot product) before creating the collection and treat it as immutable; changing it requires recreating the collection and re-ingesting all data.
rule.rationale: Vector DB indexes are built for a specific metric. Using the wrong metric produces incorrect similarity rankings; correcting it is a full re-index.

rule.statement: Validate embedding dimensions match the collection definition before ingesting; dimension mismatches cause silent failures in some clients.
rule.rationale: Some clients silently truncate or reject mismatched vectors. Catching this at ingestion time prevents a corrupt index.

example.title: Pinecone setup
example.code:
from pinecone import Pinecone, ServerlessSpec import os
pc = Pinecone(api_key=os.environ["PINECONE_API_KEY"])
def create_index(name: str, dimension: int = 1536): if name not in pc.list_indexes().names(): pc.create_index(name=name, dimension=dimension, metric="cosine", spec=ServerlessSpec(cloud="aws", region="us-east-1")) return pc.Index(name)
index = create_index("my-index") # Upsert in batches of 100 index.upsert(vectors=[{"id": "1", "values": emb, "metadata": {"source": "a.pdf"}}]) results = index.query(vector=query_emb, top_k=5, include_metadata=True)

example.title: Chroma local dev
example.code: import chromadb client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db") col = client.get_or_create_collection("documents", metadata={"hnsw:space": "cosine"}) col.add(ids=["1"], embeddings=[emb], documents=["text"], metadatas=[{"source": "a.pdf"}]) results = col.query(query_embeddings=[qemb], n_results=5, include=["documents", "distances"])

example.title: Qdrant production self-hosted
example.code:
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient from qdrant_client.models import VectorParams, Distance, PointStruct
client = QdrantClient(host="localhost", port=6333) client.create_collection("documents", vectors_config=VectorParams(size=1536, distance=Distance.COSINE)) client.upsert("documents", points=[PointStruct(id=1, vector=emb, payload={"text": "..."})]) results = client.search("documents", query_vector=qemb, limit=5)

example.title: Weaviate setup
example.code:
import weaviate from weaviate.classes.config import Configure, Property, DataType
client = weaviate.connect_to_local() client.collections.create( name="Documents", vectorizer_config=Configure.Vectorizer.text2vec_openai(), properties=[Property(name="content", data_type=DataType.TEXT)], ) col = client.collections.get("Documents") with col.batch.dynamic() as batch: batch.add_object(properties={"content": "..."}) results = col.query.near_text(query="search", limit=5)

example.title: pgvector PostgreSQL
example.code:
import psycopg2 from pgvector.psycopg2 import register_vector
conn = psycopg2.connect(host="localhost", database="vectordb", user="user", password="password") register_vector(conn) cur = conn.cursor() cur.execute("CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector") cur.execute(""" CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS documents ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, content TEXT, embedding vector(1536) ) """) cur.execute("CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS emb_idx ON documents USING ivfflat (embedding vector_cosine_ops) WITH (lists=100)") conn.commit() # Insert cur.execute("INSERT INTO documents (content, embedding) VALUES (%s, %s)", (text, emb)) # Search cur.execute("SELECT id, content, 1-(embedding <=> %s) AS sim FROM documents ORDER BY embedding <=> %s LIMIT 5", (qemb, qemb))

example.title: VectorStoreFactory abstraction

antipattern.description: Storing full document text as metadata (kilobytes per record).
antipattern.reason: Metadata is loaded into memory at query time. Large payloads bloat RAM and slow query throughput. Store text externally; keep only IDs and small filter fields in metadata.

antipattern.description: Using a cloud managed DB (Pinecone Serverless) for hard real-time SLAs without testing cold-start latency.
antipattern.reason: Serverless instances have cold-start latency on the first query after idle periods. Not suitable for sub-100ms P99 requirements.

antipattern.description: Not implementing a document update/delete strategy before bulk ingestion.
antipattern.reason: Vector DBs have no referential integrity. Deleted source documents leave stale chunks in the index. Plan delete+re-insert or soft-delete tracking before the first ingest.

antipattern.description: Agents calling delete_collection without human confirmation.
antipattern.reason: There is no undo. Collection deletion must always require human confirmation.

antipattern.description: pgvector IVFFlat index without VACUUM ANALYZE after bulk inserts.
antipattern.reason: The query planner will not use the index without VACUUM ANALYZE; agents running bulk loads must include this step.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: An agent selects the appropriate vector store backend based on constraints (self-hosted vs managed, vector count estimate, filter complexity), provisions the collection with the correct schema and index type, then runs an ingestion loop. A second agent validates the setup by querying with known embeddings and asserting top-1 recall matches expected results. Schema selection and metric choice are human-confirmed before the collection is created.

content.prompt: You are a vector store provisioner. Given: provider (pinecone|chroma|qdrant|weaviate|pgvector), vector_dim, distance_metric, filterable_fields, expected_vector_count. Steps: 1. Select index type based on expected_vector_count (<10K→Flat, <1M→HNSW, >1M→IVF or HNSW+quantization). 2. Provision the collection/index. 3. Create indexes for each filterable_field. 4. Return: {"provider": "...", "collection": "...", "index_type": "...", "status": "ready"}

list.item: Validate embedding dimension against collection config at startup — raise early rather than silently upsert wrong-dim vectors
Choose HNSW for latency-sensitive production search; IVF only if RAM is the binding constraint
Store raw text content in metadata/payload alongside the embedding — agents need to return human-readable context, not just IDs
Keep metadata fields small (KB scale); store large blobs in S3/GCS and put only the object key in metadata
Use consistent, deterministic IDs derived from document hash so re-ingestion is idempotent (upsert, not insert)
Test distance metric choice with a 100-sample labeled set before committing to a schema
For multi-tenant apps, use separate namespaces (Pinecone) or collections (Qdrant/Chroma) per tenant rather than metadata filtering — prevents cross-tenant data leaks
